era_context: The Thirty Years' War is in its second decade, with Swedish intervention beginning under Gustav II Adolf; the Ming Dynasty in China faces severe internal and external crises; the Ottoman Empire consolidates control over the eastern Mediterranean; European colonial expansion accelerates in the Americas; the Scientific Revolution advances with figures like Descartes and Galileo.

events.title: Sack of Magdeburg
events.description: Imperial and Saxon forces under Count Tilly besieged and sacked the Protestant city of Magdeburg on 20 May 1631. The city was largely destroyed, and the population was decimated—estimates range from 20,000 to 40,000 deaths out of a population of ~25,000–30,000. The sack was one of the most destructive events of the Thirty Years' War and shocked Protestant Europe. It prompted Swedish King Gustav II Adolf to intervene directly in the war.
key_figures: Count Johann Tserclaes von Tilly (Imperial commander)
Gottfried Heinrich, Count of Pappenheim (Imperial general)
Christian William, Archbishop-Administrator of Magdeburg (city leader)

sources.name: Contemporary accounts from Imperial, Saxon, and Swedish sources
Pufendorf's Historia Belli Suecici and other contemporary chronicles
Archaeological evidence from excavations in Magdeburg

events.certainty_note: The date, location, and general outcome are well-documented. Casualty figures vary widely in sources and remain debated.

events.title: Battle of Breitenfeld (preliminary engagement)
events.description: Swedish King Gustav II Adolf, responding to the sack of Magdeburg, engaged Imperial forces under Count Tilly at the Battle of Breitenfeld on 17 September 1631. This was the first major engagement between Swedish and Imperial forces. Though the battle was tactically complex, the Swedish-Saxon alliance achieved a strategic victory, demonstrating the effectiveness of Gustav's military innovations and establishing Sweden as a major military power in the Thirty Years' War.
key_figures: Count Johann Tserclaes von Tilly (Imperial commander)
John George I, Elector of Saxony (Saxon ally)

sources.name: Contemporary Swedish, Imperial, and Saxon military records
Pufendorf's Historia Belli Suecici and other contemporary chronicles

events.certainty_note: The date, location, and outcome are precisely documented in multiple contemporary military sources.

events.title: Descartes publishes Discourse on Method
events.region: Dutch Golden Age (Netherlands, published in Leiden)
events.description: René Descartes published his Discourse on Method (Discourse de la Méthode) in 1637 (though some sources date preliminary work to 1631). This philosophical and methodological work laid the foundation for modern philosophy and the scientific method, introducing the famous principle 'Cogito, ergo sum' (I think, therefore I am). The work represented a major shift toward rationalism and empiricism in European thought.
events.certainty_note: The Discourse on Method was published in 1637, not 1631. This entry may conflate preliminary work or planning with the actual publication.

events.title: Ming Dynasty: Li Zicheng's rebellion gains momentum
events.description: Li Zicheng, a former postal worker turned rebel leader, led a major peasant rebellion against Ming Dynasty rule. By 1631, his forces had grown significantly, exploiting widespread discontent over taxation, famine, and military failures. Li's rebellion would eventually contribute to the Ming Dynasty's collapse in 1644, as the Chongzhen Emperor was forced to divert resources from defending against the Qing Dynasty.

sources.name: Ming imperial records and court documents
Chinese chronicles and later Qing histories

events.certainty_note: Li Zicheng's rebellion is well-documented in Ming imperial records and Chinese chronicles.

events.title: Ottoman-Safavid War: Continued conflict over Mesopotamia
events.description: The Ottoman Empire under Murad IV continued military campaigns against the Safavid Dynasty for control of Mesopotamia and the Caucasus. These conflicts were part of the long-standing Ottoman-Safavid rivalry that had defined Middle Eastern geopolitics for over a century. The Ottoman campaigns would culminate in the capture of Baghdad in 1638.

sources.name: Ottoman imperial records and military chronicles
Safavid chronicles and later Ottoman histories

events.certainty_note: The Ottoman-Safavid conflict is well-documented, but specific events in 1631 are less clearly recorded than the major campaigns of 1638.

events.title: Tokugawa shogunate: Sakoku policy enforcement
events.description: The Tokugawa shogunate under Tokugawa Iemitsu continued to enforce the sakoku (closed country) policy, restricting foreign contact and Christian missionary activity. By 1631, the policy was becoming increasingly strict, with severe penalties for Christian conversion and foreign trade. This policy would remain in effect for over two centuries, isolating Japan from most foreign influence.
key_figures: Tokugawa Ieyasu (founder, still influential through legacy)
events.certainty_note: The sakoku policy is well-documented in Tokugawa records and contemporary foreign accounts.

events.title: English colonization of Barbados
events.description: English colonists under Sir William Courteen and Sir Peter Hay established a settlement on the island of Barbados in 1627 (though some sources date the first permanent settlement to 1631). Barbados would become one of the most important English colonies in the Caribbean, developing a plantation economy based on sugar production and enslaved African labor. The island's development represented the expansion of English colonial power and the emergence of the Atlantic slave trade.
events.certainty_note: The founding date is variously given as 1627 or 1631 in different sources. The 1627 date is more commonly used in modern scholarship.

disconfirming_evidence: The Discourse on Method was published in 1637, not 1631. The founding of Barbados is variously dated to 1627 or 1631; modern scholarship generally favors 1627. The casualty figures for the Sack of Magdeburg are highly disputed, with estimates ranging from 20,000 to 40,000 deaths, but some modern historians argue the figures were exaggerated in contemporary accounts.
historiographic_note: 1631 is exceptionally well-documented for European events, particularly the Thirty Years' War, with abundant primary sources from multiple sides. The Sack of Magdeburg is one of the most thoroughly documented events of the war, though casualty figures remain debated. Asian sources (Ming, Qing, Japanese) are substantial but reflect official court perspectives. Colonial sources are limited and heavily biased toward European perspectives; indigenous and enslaved African viewpoints are largely absent. The Ottoman-Safavid conflict is documented primarily from Ottoman sources, with Safavid perspectives underrepresented.

graph_edges.note: The Sack of Magdeburg prompted Swedish intervention and the Battle of Breitenfeld
Breitenfeld's success established Sweden as a major military power and led to further campaigns
Li Zicheng's rebellion contributed to the Ming Dynasty's collapse
